region_of_origin: Ipoh, Perak, Malaysia; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ipoh Hor Fun; closest defensible match is the generic rice noodle dish class FOODON_03315595 used as a parent-level anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice noodles, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a broad flat rice noodle dish with toppings and gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Ipoh Hor Fun as a composite dish; A0FHR (rice noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor. Composite dish facets are not available in the public FoodEx2 browser for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Ipoh Hor Fun or broad flat rice noodle dishes as of 2026.

main_ingredients: chicken or pork bone broth gravy

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-plate serving of Ipoh Hor Fun is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous bed of flat rice noodles topped with char siew, shredded chicken, braised mushrooms, and green vegetables, finished with a ladle of savoury gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Ipoh Hor Fun — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium) sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data at confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. Cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were supplemented from a secondary commercial nutritional panel at confidence 0.70; these values are flagged accordingly in notes. Iron was reported as -1 (not available) in the HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; flat rice noodles analogous entry, GI ~61 referenced to glucose
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Ipoh Hor Fun as a composite dish. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked flat rice noodles (hor fun / fresh rice noodles), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 14.7 g/100g × 450 g serving / 100) ≈ 40.8. The composite dish with protein from chicken and char siew and fibre from vegetables may modestly lower the effective GI in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 365 mg/100 g; a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,643 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 800 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and gravy, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and pair the dish with additional unseasoned vegetables to partially offset the sodium load; regular consumption at full portion is not advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles are a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~61); a 450 g serving delivers approximately 66 g of carbohydrate with only 6.75 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~40, which is clinically high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request extra protein (chicken, prawns) and vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, avoid sweet dark soy sauce, and monitor post-meal blood glucose when consuming this dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 1.78 g/100 g and cholesterol is 55.56 mg/100 g; a full 450 g serving contributes approximately 8 g saturated fat and 250 mg cholesterol, approaching daily limits for CVD patients; sodium is also elevated.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request lean chicken only (omitting char siew), ask for reduced gravy and soy sauce to lower saturated fat and sodium, limit portion to 250 g, and choose this dish only occasionally as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.78 g/100 g), minimal trans fat (0.04 g/100 g), and the presence of anti-inflammatory vegetables (choy sum, bean sprouts) make this dish relatively suitable; however, nightshade sensitivity and high sodium are relevant cautions.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine should request reduced chilli; all patients should request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ipoh Hor Fun (怡保河粉) is a celebrated Malaysian-Chinese noodle dish originating from Ipoh, Perak, Malaysia, and widely popular in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops. The dish features broad, silky flat rice noodles (hor fun) that are blanched until tender and plated with a combination of toppings: thinly sliced char siew (Chinese barbecued pork), hand-shredded poached chicken, braised black mushrooms, and blanched choy sum or bean sprouts. The dish is finished with a generous ladle of savoury, lightly thickened chicken or pork bone broth gravy seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil. The texture profile is soft and slippery from the noodles, tender from the chicken, and slightly chewy from the mushrooms. The flavour is umami-rich, mildly sweet from the char siew, and savoury from the gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 101 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 3.1 g fat (1.78 g saturated), 14.7 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 365 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 61 (medium), driven by the refined flat rice noodles. Clinically, the dish is a high concern for hypertension due to its sodium content (a 450 g serving delivers ~1,643 mg sodium), a high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycemic load per serving (~40 GL for a 450 g plate), and a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and cholesterol from char siew and gravy. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to pork and chicken content, and is not halal as it contains char siew pork. It is dairy-free, and gluten-free in its base form though soy sauce typically contains wheat. Ipoh Hor Fun is commonly consumed as a breakfast or lunch dish and is a staple of Malaysian-Chinese and Singaporean hawker culture.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data (confidence 0.95) for an exact dish match. Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) supplemented from a secondary commercial nutritional panel at lower confidence (0.70). GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (flat rice noodles). Overall confidence is high for the HPB-sourced values and medium for the supplemented values.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore source reports iron as not available for this dish entry; detailed iron data for this traditional composite preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source, consistent with the savoury preparation profile of this dish (no added sugar in the standard recipe). trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100 g is sourced from a secondary commercial nutritional panel (confidence 0.70) and should be treated as indicative only. The cultural tags in the input (primary_cuisine: western; dietary_tags: halal, vegetarian, plant_based) were identified as incorrect and have been corrected: Ipoh Hor Fun is a Malaysian-Chinese dish, is not vegetarian (contains pork and chicken), and is not halal (contains char siew pork). The portion size of 450 g is retained from the input as a reasonable estimate for a standard hawker plate. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite-dish GI measurement is available in the approved literature. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact codes exist for this composite dish.
